name: Kamba
description: The Akamba are a Bantu people of Eastern Kenya's semi-arid lowlands, historically renowned as long-distance traders who connected the interior with the Swahili coast. Their trade networks stretched from the interior to Mombasa and beyond, dealing in ivory, iron, beeswax, and grain. The Kamba are celebrated for intricate woodcarving and basket-weaving — crafts that became their economic signature in the colonial and post-independence eras. Music, storytelling, and athletic dance are central to social life.
cultural_highlights: Long-distance trade heritage — Akamba traders were the principal middlemen between interior Kenya and the Swahili coast through the 19th century
Woodcarving mastery — Kamba carvers produce both utilitarian and decorative objects; Wamunyu market near Machakos is a global export hub
Kilumi — a spirit possession ritual dance performed by women, considered a form of healing and spiritual communication
Mũsũlũ — a ceremony marking end of mourning where community feasts, dances, and restores balance after loss
Basket weaving — intricate sisal and grass weaving with complex geometric patterns unique to Kamba women's craft

food_traditions.name: Kimwe (mashed sorghum and beans)
food_traditions.description: Traditional everyday meal combining sorghum flour with beans or pigeon peas

food_traditions.name: Mboo (fermented sorghum gruel)
food_traditions.description: Sour fermented porridge consumed as a probiotic drink and light meal

food_traditions.name: Munyere (stewed game meat)
food_traditions.description: Slow-cooked wild meat with indigenous herbs — a traditional dry-season staple

music_traditions: Kilumi drum ensemble — large ceremonial drums accompanying the spirit-possession healing dances
Mwali — coming-of-age songs sung by young women during initiation and courtship ceremonies
Iluta — competitive acrobatic dance where young men demonstrate athletic skill and stamina
